behaviors.description: The traveling-salesman heuristic produces a route that is no more than 1.5x the length of the optimal route for inputs up to 20 stops.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is the heuristic algorithm’s output quality versus known-optimal baselines, which can be tested in-process with deterministic coordinate fixtures. No OS/network/subprocess dependency, no AI model dependency, no multi-agent sequence. Higher levels were rejected because behavior text is only about route-length ratio, not service handoff or external API behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Run a pure algorithm test harness over a fixed hard-case canary set (clustered points, outliers, near-collinear, symmetric traps) plus seeded random cases; compare heuristic route length to precomputed optimal lengths for each fixture and assert ratio <= 1.5.

behaviors.description: The delivery time window estimates are computed correctly from the distance between consecutive stops and the configured average speed for the road type.
behaviors.justification: Real failure is likely at module handoff: leg-distance output, road-type speed lookup, and estimator composition (including unit normalization). In-process wiring of these modules is needed; pure single-function unit tests can miss conversion/lookup boundary issues. Integration brake checks: no real OS/network/subprocess needed, no AI output dependency, no multi-step agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Wire route-leg distance provider + road-type speed config + time-window estimator in one in-process test; use fixtures with mixed road types and known distances, then assert per-leg and cumulative ETA windows match expected values (including unit conversion cases).

behaviors.description: The shift-hours validator rejects a route whose total estimated time exceeds the driver's remaining shift hours, returning the excess amount.
behaviors.justification: Behavior is local validation math and threshold logic on provided totals. Failure does not require module interaction beyond function/object boundary, and does not involve real infra or AI output. Higher levels were rejected because behavior text is strictly about reject/return-excess logic.
behaviors.test_description: Call validator with deterministic inputs for estimated route time and remaining shift hours; assert rejection when exceeded and exact excess amount returned, including edge cases (equal, just over, rounding boundaries).

behaviors.description: When one address in the list fails geocoding (API returns no results), the service skips that stop and optimizes the remaining addresses, reporting which addresses were skipped.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at cross-module orchestration boundary: geocode results feed filtering, optimization input, and skipped-address reporting. A deterministic stub geocoder returning "no results" for one address is sufficient to reproduce this specific behavior. System was considered but rejected because behavior text specifies semantic API outcome (no results), not real network/timeout/HTTP transport conditions.
behaviors.test_description: In-process integration test with a fake geocoder client: one address returns empty result, others return coordinates; assert skipped list contains failed address, optimizer receives only successful stops, and final route excludes skipped stop while remaining stops are optimized.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in the input list are consolidated into a single stop with combined delivery items, not visited twice.
behaviors.justification: Real risk is data handoff across deduplication, item aggregation, and route-construction modules; a unit test on dedupe alone can miss double-visit reintroduction downstream. No real OS/network/subprocess requirement, no AI dependency, no multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Wire input normalization + delivery-item aggregation + route builder; provide duplicates with distinct item payloads and assert one consolidated stop in route, aggregated items preserved, and visit count is exactly one for that address.
